volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 2.3.305 - Section 3.3.426)

sections.label: Section 2.3.305
sections.original: subducunt lembum capitibus quassantibus .
sections.translation: shaking their heads , they hauled their bark ashore . We deposited all the gold with Theotimus , who is a priest there [*] of Ephesian Diana .

sections.label: Section 2.3.308
sections.original: Quis istíc Theotimust ?
sections.translation: Who is this Theotimus ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.308b
sections.original: Megalobuli filius ,
sections.translation: The son of Megalobyzus [*] , who is now living at Ephesus , a man most dear to the Ephesians .

sections.label: Section 2.3.310
sections.original: Ne ille hercle mihi sit multo tanto carior ,
sections.translation: By my troth , he ’ ll surely turn out very much more dear to me [*] if he shall chouse me out of all that gold .

sections.label: Section 2.3.312
sections.original: Quin in eapse aede Dianai conditumst ;
sections.translation: But it is stowed away in the very temple itself of the Goddess Diana ; there they keep guard at the public expense [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.3.313b
sections.original: Occidistis me ;
sections.translation: You kill me outright ; much more safely would it have been kept in private hands .

sections.label: Section 2.3.315
sections.original: sed nilne áttulistis inde auri domum ?
sections.translation: But haven ’ t you brought any of the gold home here ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.316
sections.original: Immo etiam . verum quantum attulerit nescio .
sections.translation: O yes ; but how much he has brought , I don ’ t know .

sections.label: Section 2.3.317
sections.original: Quid ? nescis ?
sections.translation: How ’ s that ?— not know ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.317b
sections.original: Quia Mnesilochus noctu clanculum
sections.translation: Because Mnesilochus went privately by night to Theotimus ; and didn ’ t wish to entrust it to me , nor to any one in the ship . For that reason I don ’ t know

sections.label: Section 2.3.320
sections.original: quantillum attulerit ; verum haud permultum attulit .
sections.translation: how much he has brought , but he hasn ’ t brought very much .

sections.label: Section 2.3.321
sections.original: Etiam dimidium censes ?
sections.translation: Do you think it was even a half ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.321b
sections.original: Non edepol scio ;
sections.translation: By my troth , I know not ; but I don ’ t think it .

sections.label: Section 2.3.322b
sections.original: Fertne partem tertiam ?
sections.translation: Does he bring a third part ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.323
sections.original: Non hercle opinor ; verum verum nescio .
sections.translation: Troth , I think not ; but I do not know the truth . Indeed , I know nothing at all about the gold , except that I do know nothing about it .

sections.label: Section 2.3.325
sections.original: nunc tibimet illuc navi capiundumst iter ,
sections.translation: Now , you yourself must take a voyage there on board ship , to carry home this gold from Theotimus ; and , hearkye —

sections.label: Section 2.3.327b
sections.original: Quid vis ?
sections.translation: What do you want ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.327c
sections.original: Anulum gnati tui
sections.translation: Take care and remember to carry your son ’ s ring .

sections.label: Section 2.3.328b
sections.original: Quid opust anulo ?
sections.translation: What need is there of the ring ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.329
sections.original: Quia id sígnumst cum Theotimo , quí eum illi adferet ,
sections.translation: Because that is the token agreed on with Theotimus , that the person who brings it to him ,

sections.label: Section 2.3.330
sections.original: ei aurum ut reddat .
sections.translation: to him he is to give up the gold .

sections.label: Section 2.3.330b
sections.original: Meminero , et recte mones .
sections.translation: I ’ ll remember it , and you advise me well . But is this Theotimus rich ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.331b
sections.original: Etiam rogas ?
sections.translation: What , do you ask that ? Why , hasn ’ t he the soles fastened to his shoes with gold ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.333
sections.original: Cur ita fastidit ?
sections.translation: Why does he thus despise it ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.333b
sections.original: Tantas divitias habet ;
sections.translation: He has such immense riches ; he doesn ’ t know what to do with his gold .

sections.label: Section 2.3.334b
sections.original: Mihi dederit velim .
sections.translation: I wish he ’ d give it me .

sections.label: Section 2.3.335
sections.original: sed qui praesente id aurum Theotimo datumst ?
sections.translation: But in whose presence was this gold delivered to Theotimus ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.336
sections.original: Populo praesente : nullust Ephesi quin sciat .
sections.translation: In presence of the people ; there ’ s not a person in Ephesus but what knows it .

sections.label: Section 2.3.337
sections.original: Istuc sapienter saltem fecit filius ,
sections.translation: My son at least did wisely there , in entrusting that gold to a rich man to keep . From him it may be recovered even at a moment ’ s notice .

sections.label: Section 2.3.340
sections.original: Immo em tantisper numquam te morabitur ,
sections.translation: Aye , and look here , he ’ ll never keep you waiting even thus much ( shows his finger-nail ) from receiving it on the very day you arrive there .

sections.label: Section 2.3.342
sections.original: Censebam me effugisse a vita marituma ,
sections.translation: I thought that I had escaped a seafaring life , and that , at length , an old man of my years , I shouldn ’ t be sailing about . But now I find that I ’ m not allowed to have the choice whether I would or no ;

sections.label: Section 2.3.345
sections.original: ita bellus hospes fecit Archidemides .
sections.translation: ’ tis my fine host Archidemides has done me this . Where , then , is my son , Mnesilochus , at present ?

sections.label: Section 2.3.347
sections.original: Deos átque amicos iit salutatum ad forum .
sections.translation: He has gone to the market-place to pay his respects to the Deities and his friends .

sections.label: Section 2.3.348
sections.original: At ego hinc ad illum , ut convenam quantum potest .—
sections.translation: Then I ’ ll go hence to meet him as soon as I can .

sections.label: Section 2.3.349
sections.original: Ille est oneratus recte et plus iusto vehit .
sections.translation: He ’ s right well loaded , and carries more than his proper burden .

sections.label: Section 2.3.350
sections.original: exorsa haec tela non male omnino mihi est :
sections.translation: Not so very badly has this web been commenced by me , that I may find means for the son of my master in his amour . I ’ ve managed it so , that he may take as much of the gold as he chooses , and give up to his father as much as he may like to give up . The old gentleman will be going hence to Ephesus to fetch his gold ;

sections.label: Section 2.3.355
sections.original: hic nostra agetur aetas in malacum modum ,
sections.translation: here our life will be spent in a delicious manner , since the old man will leave me and Mnesilochus here , and not be taking us along with him . O ! what a kick-up I shall be making here . But what ’ s to be done , when the old gentleman shall have discovered this ? When he shall have found out that he has made his journey thither to no purpose ,

sections.label: Section 2.3.360
sections.original: nosque aurum abusos ? quid mihi fiet postea ?
sections.translation: and that we have misspent his gold , what will become of me after that ? I ’ faith , I think upon his arrival he ’ ll be changing my name , and at once be making me Crucisalus [*] instead of Chrysalus . Troth , I ’ ll run away , if there shall be a greater necessity for it . If I ’ m caught , I ’ ll plague him for a punishment [*] ;

sections.label: Section 2.3.365
sections.original: si illi sunt virgae ruri , at mihi tergum domist .
sections.translation: if his rods are in the fields , still my back ’ s at home . Now I ’ ll be off , and tell my master ’ s son this contrivance about the gold , and about his mistress Bacchis who has been found .

sections.label: Section 3.1.368
sections.original: Pándite atque aperíte propere iánuam hanc Orci , óbsecro .
sections.translation: Open and throw back straightway this gate of hell [*] , I do entreat . For , really , I do deem it nothing else ; inasmuch as no one comes here

sections.label: Section 3.1.370
sections.original: nisi quem spes reliquere omnes , esse ut frugi possiet .
sections.translation: but he whon all hopes have deserted , that he may yet be a decent person . Bacchis — no , not Bacchis is their name , but they are most determined Bacchanals . Avaunt from me , you sisters , who suck the blood of men . Richly and elegantly furnished is this house — for destruction . Soon as I beheld all this , at once I straight-way way betook myself to my heels .

sections.label: Section 3.1.375
sections.original: egone ut haec conclusa gestem clanculum ? ut celem patrem ,
sections.translation: And ought I to carry these things concealed in secrecy ? Ought I , Pistoclerus , to conceal from your father your excesses , or your misdeeds , or your places of resort ? by which you are aiming to drive your father and myself , and your own self and all your friends , to disgrace , and shame , and ruin altogether , and to destroy us all . Within yourself you entertain no awe of either me or your own self on account of the deeds which you are doing

sections.label: Section 3.1.380
sections.original: quibus tuom patrem meque una , amicos , adfinis tuos
sections.translation: by which you have made your own father and myself as well , your friends and your connexions , to be abettors [*] in the disgrace of your excesses . Now , before you add this mischief to the rest , I am resolved at once to tell your parent . This instant from myself will I remove this blame , and shall disclose the matter to the old gentleman , that he may forthwith draw forth his son out from this loathsome pollution .

sections.label: Section 3.2.385
sections.original: Múltimodis meditatus egomet mecum sum , et ita esse arbitror :
sections.translation: In many ways have I thought it over with myself , and thus I think it is ; a man your friend , who is a friend such as the name imports — except the Gods — nothing does excel him . By fact have I experienced it so to be . For when I departed hence to Ephesus (’ tis now almost two years ago it happened ), from Ephesus I sent letters hither to my friend Pistoclerus ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.390
sections.original: misi , amicam ut mi inveniret Bacchidem . illum intellego
sections.translation: requesting that he would find out my mistress Bacchis for me . I hear that he has found her , as my servant Chrysalus has brought me word . How aptly , too , has he framed a device against my father about the gold , that I may have abundance in my amour . I see ’ tis right [*] that I should make a due return . ’ Tis better for you to be styled extravagant than ungrateful ;

sections.label: Section 3.2.395
sections.original: malefactorem amitti satius quam relinqui beneficum ;
sections.translation: but , i ’ faith , in my way of thinking at least , there ’ s nothing more extravagant than the ungrateful man . The former the good will praise , the latter even the bad will censure . ’ Twere better for an ill-doer to escape than for a benefactor to be deserted . For this reason , then , it behoves me to take the greater care ; I needs must be on the watch . Now , Mnesilochus , the sample is on view , now the contest is being decided ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.400
sections.original: sisne necne ut esse oportet , malus , bonus quoivis modi ,
sections.translation: whether you are or are not such as you ought to be ; good or bad , of whichever kind ; just or unjust , penurious or liberal , fretful or complying . Take you care , if you please , lest you let your servant exce . you in doing well . Whatever you shall prove , I warn you , you shall not be concealed . But see , I perceive my friend ’ s father and his tutor coming this way . I ’ ll listen what matter ’ tis they are upon .

sections.label: Section 3.3.405
sections.original: Nunc experiar , sitne aceto tibi cor acre in pectore .
sections.translation: I ’ ll now make trial , whether your heart is sharpened by wisdom in your breast . Follow me .

sections.label: Section 3.3.406b
sections.original: Quo sequar ? quo ducis nunc me ?
sections.translation: Whither shall I follow ? Whither are you now leading me ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.406c
sections.original: Ad illam quae tuom
sections.translation: To her who has undone , utterly destroyed your single , only son .

sections.label: Section 3.3.408
sections.original: Heia , Lyde , leniter qui saeviunt sapiunt magis .
sections.translation: How now , Lydus ; those are the wiser who moderate their passion . ’ Tis less to be [*] wondered at if this age does some of these things

sections.label: Section 3.3.410
sections.original: quam si non faciat . feci égo istaec itidem in adulescentia .
sections.translation: than if it does not do so ; I , as well , did the same in my youth .

sections.label: Section 3.3.411
sections.original: Ei mihi , ei mihi , istaec illum perdidit assentatio .
sections.translation: Ah me ! ah me ! this over-indulgence has proved his ruin . For had he been without you , I should have had him trained up to moral rectitude ; now , by reason of you and your trusting disposition , Pistoclerus has become abandoned .

sections.label: Section 3.3.414b
sections.original: Di immortales , meum sodalem hic nominat .
sections.translation: Immortal Gods ! he names my friend .

sections.label: Section 3.3.415
sections.original: quid hoc negoti est , Pistoclerum Lydus quod erum tam ciet ?
sections.translation: What means this , that Lydus is thus exciting his master , Pistoclerus ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.416
sections.original: Paulisper , Lyde , est libido hómini suo animo obsequi ;
sections.translation: ’ Tis but a little time , Lydus , that a man has a desire to indulge his inclinations ; the time will soon come , when he will hate himself even . Humour him ; so that care is taken that he offends not beyond the line of honor , e ’ en suffer him .

sections.label: Section 3.3.419
sections.original: Non sino , neque equidem illum me vivo corrumpi sinam .
sections.translation: I ’ ll suffer him not , nor , for my part , while I ’ m alive , will I allow him to be corrupted .

sections.label: Section 3.3.420
sections.original: sed tu , qui pro tam corrupto dicis causam filio ,
sections.translation: But you , who are pleading his cause for a son so profligate , was this same your own training , when you were a young man ? I declare that for your first twenty years you had not even this much liberty , to move your foot out of the house even a finger ’ s length away from your tutor .

sections.label: Section 3.3.424
sections.original: ante solem exorientém nisi in palaestram veneras ,
sections.translation: Before the rising of the sun had you not come to the school for exercise [*] , no slight punishment would you have had at the hands of the master of the school .

sections.label: Section 3.3.426
sections.original: id quom optigerat , hoc etiam ad malum accersebatur malum :
sections.translation: When it did happen so , this evil , too , was added to the evil ; both pupil and preceptor were esteemed disgraced .
